S3 ESP32-S3 · Rust & Elixir Contents Cheatsheet
◉ A BookBank field guide

Programming the ESP32-S3

A from-first-principles field guide to a $5 dual-core radio-on-a-chip — what it is, what to build with it, how to write its firmware in Rust, and how to wire it to a fleet-scale Elixir backend.

narrated in the voice of Richard Feynman · first-principles, intuition-first

▶ Start reading ⚡ Cheatsheet

Before you turn the page

There is something magical about a chip you can hold on a fingertip that talks to Wi-Fi, runs two processors at 240 MHz, and costs less than a sandwich. This book takes one honest question — "how do I actually program one of these, and connect it to a real backend?" — and answers it from the ground up.

We won't hand-wave. We start with what the silicon really is, decide what it's good for, pick a language with eyes open, and then write firmware in Rust that streams readings to an Elixir server built to hold thousands of these little radios at once. Read the chapters in order — each one is a rung, and the next one stands on it.

🧭
Three threads, one rope

Your question had three strands — the board, Rust on it, and an Elixir backend. We follow each, then braid them together in the final chapter.

Table of Contents

  1. The Big Picture Your question, answered in one breath: board, Rust, Elixir — and the journey ahead. overview
  2. Meet the ESP32-S3 Two cores, a radio, 45 pins, and AI vector math — what's really inside. the chip
  3. What Can You Build? From a soil sensor to an on-device voice assistant — matching ideas to the silicon. use cases
  4. Choosing Your Language ESP-IDF/C, Arduino, MicroPython, Rust — the honest trade-offs. tooling
  5. Rust on the ESP32 no_std vs std, the Xtensa toolchain, and your first blink. rust
  6. Wi-Fi, MQTT & the Outside World Joining a network and publishing readings over the protocol IoT runs on. networking
  7. The Elixir Backend Why the BEAM loves IoT: a broker, Tortoise, PubSub, and a live dashboard. backend
  8. End-to-End Blueprint Rust firmware → MQTT → Phoenix. The whole rope, in one diagram and a checklist. build
🔑
About this book

Researched from Espressif's official documentation and developer portal, the Rust on ESP book and the esp-rs project, and the Elixir/Phoenix & Tortoise docs. Firmware facts are verified against esp-hal 1.0 (the first vendor-backed Rust SDK, stabilized 30 Oct 2025) and the esp-idf-svc std crates. Everything renders offline; there are no network dependencies in the finished book.

🍎
A note on the voice

I've tried to explain everything the way I'd want it explained to me: start from something you already know, build the idea up brick by brick, and never hide behind a fancy word when a plain one will do. If a thing is genuinely hard, I'll say so. The fun is in understanding — not in memorizing. So let's go find out how these little machines really work.

Press ⌘R or reopen the book to refresh after edits.
